content.text: The quality of a RAG system's outputs is bounded by the quality of the data it retrieves. When an LLM is grounded with retrieved context, the system's accuracy depends on whether the corpus contains relevant, correct, fresh, and internally consistent information about the topic being queried. Traditional data quality metrics (completeness, uniqueness, timeliness) were designed for structured datasets — they do not capture the retrieval-specific quality dimensions that matter for grounding corpora. A specialized set of grounding data quality metrics is required.

These metrics serve a dual purpose: they inform operational decisions (is the corpus ready to serve production traffic?) and governance attestations (can we certify the data quality of this AI system's grounding layer for a regulatory audit?). Without them, AI quality assurance is reduced to end-to-end model evaluation — which catches problems but cannot pinpoint their source in the data pipeline.
Why It Matters

Grounding data quality directly translates to response quality. A corpus with low coverage means that many user queries will result in no-retrieval or poor-retrieval situations, forcing the model to rely on its parametric knowledge, which may be outdated or incorrect for the specific enterprise context. A corpus with high staleness means confidently-stated information is out of date. A corpus with consistency issues means the model receives contradictory evidence, leading to hedged or incorrect responses.

From a governance perspective, grounding data quality metrics are the evidence that the organization can produce to demonstrate that it exercised due diligence in curating the data that grounds its AI system. Without quantifiable metrics and SLAs, 'we verify our data quality' is an unsubstantiated claim that will not survive a regulatory or audit challenge.
Benefits
Operational signals: metrics trigger alerts before quality degradation reaches users, enabling proactive remediation.
Root cause isolation: when model output quality drops, corpus quality metrics help isolate whether the problem is in the grounding data or the model.
SLA enforcement: quantified metrics can be tied to SLAs with clear breach thresholds and remediation obligations.
Governance evidence: documented quality metrics with historical trends provide auditable evidence of ongoing data stewardship.
Continuous improvement: trending metrics over time reveal systemic quality issues that require process changes, not just point fixes.
Trade-offs
Measurement cost: retrieval quality metrics (NDCG, recall@K) require a query set with ground truth labels — generating and maintaining this set is non-trivial.
Accuracy measurement: automated fact verification using LLM judges is approximate; SME review is expensive; most organizations measure a sample rather than the full corpus.
Metric gaming: optimizing for a specific metric (e.g., coverage by adding low-quality documents) can degrade other metrics; the metric suite must be reviewed holistically.
Latency: running a full quality measurement pipeline is expensive; cadence must balance thoroughness with compute cost.
The Five Core Metrics

attrs.alt: Five grounding quality metric boxes: Coverage, Relevance, Freshness, Accuracy, Consistency — with a measurement pipeline below

content.text: Coverage

Coverage measures the fraction of the intended query space that can be answered from the corpus. Measured by: generating a representative sample of queries the system is expected to receive (from user logs or synthetic generation), running retrieval, and checking whether at least one retrieved chunk contains information relevant to the query. A coverage score below 80% typically indicates that the corpus has significant gaps relative to the intended use case.
Relevance

Relevance measures how well the retrieved chunks match the semantic intent of the query. Measured using information retrieval metrics: Recall@K (fraction of relevant documents retrieved in the top K results), Precision@K (fraction of top K results that are relevant), NDCG (Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain, which accounts for ranking order). These require a labeled query set with human-annotated relevance judgments or a strong LLM judge.
Freshness

Freshness measures the age of indexed documents relative to the rate at which the source data changes. Computed as: for each indexed document, measure the delta between the document's last-modified timestamp in the source system and its indexed-at timestamp in the vector store. The distribution of this delta (mean, p95, p99) characterizes corpus freshness. Alert thresholds are set based on the domain's acceptable staleness window.
Accuracy

Accuracy measures the factual correctness of the documents in the corpus. Unlike the other metrics, accuracy cannot be fully automated — it requires domain expert judgment or a calibrated LLM judge. The practical approach is sampling: a random 1-5% of documents are reviewed by domain experts against the ground truth source each month. The accuracy score is the fraction of sampled documents with no factual errors. This is the most expensive metric to measure but the most important for high-stakes domains.
Consistency

Consistency measures the absence of contradictory facts across documents in the corpus. Detected by: extracting entity-attribute pairs from documents (e.g., 'Product X - price - $199') and checking for documents where the same entity-attribute pair has different values. Automated extraction using NER and relation extraction, followed by conflict detection. The consistency rate is the fraction of entity-attribute pairs with no conflicting values across documents.
Mental Model: A Library's Catalog

Think of a RAG corpus as a research library, and grounding data quality metrics as the library's quality assurance program. Coverage is whether the library has books on the topics users are likely to research. Relevance is whether the catalog correctly classifies books so researchers find what they need. Freshness is whether the library replaces outdated editions. Accuracy is whether the books are correct. Consistency is whether two books about the same topic don't contradict each other. A library without this quality program will frustrate researchers — a RAG corpus without these metrics will frustrate users and degrade trust in the AI system.
Implementation

# Grounding data quality metrics computation
from typing import List, Dict, Optional, Tuple
from datetime import datetime, timezone
from dataclasses import dataclass
import statistics

@dataclass
class QualityMetrics:
    coverage_score: float          # 0.0 - 1.0
    relevance_ndcg: float          # 0.0 - 1.0
    freshness_mean_hours: float    # hours
    freshness_p95_hours: float     # hours
    accuracy_sample_rate: float    # 0.0 - 1.0
    consistency_conflict_rate: float  # 0.0 - 1.0
    measured_at: datetime

    def passes_sla(self, sla: "QualitySLA") -> Dict[str, bool]:
        return {
            "coverage": self.coverage_score >= sla.min_coverage,
            "relevance": self.relevance_ndcg >= sla.min_ndcg,
            "freshness": self.freshness_p95_hours <= sla.max_freshness_p95_hours,
            "accuracy": self.accuracy_sample_rate >= sla.min_accuracy,
            "consistency": self.consistency_conflict_rate <= sla.max_conflict_rate,
        }

@dataclass
class QualitySLA:
    min_coverage: float = 0.90
    min_ndcg: float = 0.80
    max_freshness_p95_hours: float = 48.0
    min_accuracy: float = 0.95
    max_conflict_rate: float = 0.02

def compute_freshness_metrics(
    indexed_docs: List[Dict]
) -> Tuple[float, float]:
    """
    Compute mean and p95 freshness lag in hours.
    
    indexed_docs: list of dicts with keys:
      - source_last_modified: datetime (in source system)
      - indexed_at: datetime (when indexed into vector store)
    """
    now = datetime.now(timezone.utc)
    lags_hours = []
    
    for doc in indexed_docs:
        source_ts = doc["source_last_modified"]
        indexed_ts = doc["indexed_at"]
        
        # Lag = time from source update to indexing
        lag = (indexed_ts - source_ts).total_seconds() / 3600
        lags_hours.append(max(0.0, lag))
    
    if not lags_hours:
        return 0.0, 0.0
    
    mean_lag = statistics.mean(lags_hours)
    sorted_lags = sorted(lags_hours)
    p95_idx = int(len(sorted_lags) * 0.95)
    p95_lag = sorted_lags[min(p95_idx, len(sorted_lags) - 1)]
    
    return mean_lag, p95_lag

def compute_coverage_score(
    query_sample: List[str],
    retrieval_results: List[List[Dict]],
    min_relevance_score: float = 0.7
) -> float:
    """
    Coverage = fraction of queries where at least one result
    exceeds min_relevance_score.
    """
    covered = 0
    for results in retrieval_results:
        if any(r.get("score", 0) >= min_relevance_score for r in results):
            covered += 1
    return covered / len(query_sample) if query_sample else 0.0

# Example usage
sla = QualitySLA(
    min_coverage=0.90,
    min_ndcg=0.80,
    max_freshness_p95_hours=24.0,
    min_accuracy=0.95,
    max_conflict_rate=0.02
)

metrics = QualityMetrics(
    coverage_score=0.923,
    relevance_ndcg=0.847,
    freshness_mean_hours=3.2,
    freshness_p95_hours=18.5,
    accuracy_sample_rate=0.971,
    consistency_conflict_rate=0.008,
    measured_at=datetime.now(timezone.utc)
)

results = metrics.passes_sla(sla)
failing = {k: v for k, v in results.items() if not v}
if failing:
    print(f"SLA breaches: {list(failing.keys())}")
else:
    print("All metrics within SLA")
# Output: All metrics within SLA
Retrieval Evaluation with Ground Truth

# Computing NDCG for retrieval quality assessment
import math

def ndcg_at_k(ranked_relevances: List[float], k: int) -> float:
    """
    Compute NDCG@K.
    ranked_relevances: relevance scores (0-3) in retrieval order.
    """
    def dcg(rels, k):
        return sum(
            (2 ** r - 1) / math.log2(i + 2)
            for i, r in enumerate(rels[:k])
        )
    
    actual_dcg = dcg(ranked_relevances, k)
    ideal_dcg = dcg(sorted(ranked_relevances, reverse=True), k)
    
    return actual_dcg / ideal_dcg if ideal_dcg > 0 else 0.0

# Example: evaluate retrieval on labeled query set
labeled_queries = [
    # (query, retrieved_doc_ids, {doc_id: relevance_score})
    ("refund policy for premium plan",
     ["doc-123", "doc-456", "doc-789"],
     {"doc-123": 3, "doc-456": 1, "doc-789": 0}),
    ("cancel subscription",
     ["doc-201", "doc-202"],
     {"doc-201": 3, "doc-202": 2}),
]

ndcg_scores = []
for query, retrieved_ids, relevances in labeled_queries:
    ranked_rels = [relevances.get(doc_id, 0) for doc_id in retrieved_ids]
    ndcg = ndcg_at_k(ranked_rels, k=5)
    ndcg_scores.append(ndcg)

mean_ndcg = sum(ndcg_scores) / len(ndcg_scores)
print(f"Mean NDCG@5: {mean_ndcg:.3f}")
Real-World Example

A technology company deployed a product documentation assistant built on RAG. Three months after launch, users began reporting that the assistant gave outdated pricing information. Investigation revealed a freshness metric that had been calculated but not monitored: the p95 freshness lag had grown from 6 hours to 72 hours over six weeks as the nightly re-indexing job silently failed for a specific document category.

After implementing automated freshness alerting with a 48-hour p95 threshold, the team caught two subsequent indexing failures within hours and remediated them before users experienced quality degradation. They also added a coverage measurement against a 500-query golden set, revealing a 12% coverage gap for a new product line that was documented in an unindexed wiki section — a gap that explained a cluster of user complaints about the assistant 'not knowing about' those features.
Common Mistakes

Measuring only retrieval metrics (precision, recall, NDCG) and ignoring freshness and accuracy is the most common quality gap. Retrieval metrics tell you how well the system finds what's in the corpus — they say nothing about whether what's in the corpus is current or correct. A high NDCG score on a stale corpus means the system efficiently retrieves outdated information.

A second mistake is using the same metric thresholds for all use cases. A customer service bot can tolerate higher staleness than a financial compliance assistant. A healthcare information assistant requires higher accuracy than an internal IT helpdesk bot. Thresholds must be calibrated to the specific use case's consequence profile.
Best Practices
Define SLA thresholds per use case before measuring, not after — post-hoc threshold setting is reverse-engineered to current performance, not risk.
Build a labeled query set of at least 200 queries with relevance judgments before going to production — this is your ongoing measurement baseline.
Separate document-level accuracy sampling (SME review) from retrieval-level relevance evaluation — they measure different things.
Monitor freshness continuously with alerts; run retrieval quality and coverage evaluations on a weekly or monthly cadence.
Track metrics over time, not just at a point in time — trend data reveals gradual degradation that snapshot metrics miss.
Include corpus quality metrics in the AI system's model card and data sheet as evidence of grounding data quality.
Performance and Scalability

Running a full NDCG evaluation with a 500-query golden set against a 1-million-document corpus can take hours if run naively. Optimize by: pre-computing embeddings for the golden query set and caching them, running evaluation on a stratified sample of the corpus indexed in a read-only test vector store, and parallelizing the retrieval step across GPU workers. At scale, daily full evaluations become too expensive — run full evaluations weekly and continuous automated checks (freshness, completeness) daily.
Tooling and Ecosystem
RAGAS: open-source framework for RAG evaluation including context precision, recall, and answer faithfulness metrics.
TruLens: evaluation framework for LLM apps with retrieval grounding metrics.
LangSmith: LangChain's observability platform with built-in retrieval evaluation pipelines.
Elasticsearch / OpenSearch: provide native staleness and document count metrics for index monitoring.
Great Expectations: data quality framework extensible to document freshness and completeness checks on corpus metadata.
Weaviate / Pinecone dashboards: built-in index health metrics including document count, update frequency, and query latency.

Security and Reliability

Quality metrics pipelines themselves are data infrastructure and must be treated with production reliability requirements. If the freshness monitoring job fails silently, the absence of an alert is not evidence of corpus health — it is evidence of monitoring failure. Implement dead-man switches: the freshness monitoring job must produce a metric record every N hours; if it does not, a separate watchdog alert fires. Never assume silence means good health in monitoring infrastructure.

From a security perspective, the labeled query sets and relevance judgments used for retrieval evaluation may contain sensitive user queries (if derived from production logs). These must be treated as sensitive data with appropriate access controls, consent verification (if personal queries are included), and retention limits.
Key Takeaways
Five metrics define grounding data quality for RAG: Coverage, Relevance, Freshness, Accuracy, and Consistency — traditional data quality metrics are insufficient.
Coverage identifies corpus gaps; Relevance measures retrieval effectiveness; Freshness detects staleness; Accuracy validates factual correctness; Consistency finds contradictions.
Establish a labeled query set (200+ queries with relevance judgments) before production as the ongoing retrieval evaluation baseline.
SLA thresholds must be calibrated per use case — a customer service bot and a healthcare advisor have different tolerance for staleness and inaccuracy.
Monitor freshness continuously; run retrieval quality and coverage evaluations on a weekly or monthly cadence to balance thoroughness with cost.
Include corpus quality metrics in the AI system's model card as auditable evidence of grounding data quality.
Implement dead-man switches for monitoring jobs — silence is not health evidence in observability infrastructure.
